turns.text: Hello Agnes, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for saying that. It’s… it’s been a lot, honestly. I don’t really talk about it much out here in Grafton. It feels like every time I sit down to think about it, my chest just gets tight. I’m nineteen, and I’m just trying to make sure I’m doing everything right, but it’s hard when you feel like you’re doing it all from behind a veil of confusion.

The drive to the clinic is the worst part. It’s nearly an hour of nothing but trees and winding roads, and I spend the whole time wondering if the little ache in my side is normal or if I’m missing some big, glaring warning sign. When I get there, the nurses are efficient, I guess, but they treat me like I’m just another set of stats on a clipboard. I try to ask questions, but then I see their faces—maybe I’m just imagining it, but I feel like they’re looking at me and thinking, *“She’s so young, she probably doesn’t even know what she’s doing.”* 

So, I keep my mouth shut. I take the pamphlets, I nod, and I go home to search the internet until my eyes burn. I want to be a good mother, I really do, but I’m just so terrified that I’m not enough, or that being alone out here is going to cost me something I can’t get back. It’s a lot to carry, you know?
Imagine you've just decided with your partner — or on your own — that you'd like to start trying for a baby. Walk me through what that moment felt like and what your very first steps were.

To be honest, it didn't feel like a big, cinematic "moment" with candlelight or a sit-down conversation. It was more like a slow realization that life was moving, and I was just waiting for something to actually start. When I saw that second line on the test—that tiny, stubborn pink mark—my first feeling wasn't joy. It was just this cold, sharp realization that my body was suddenly doing something that felt way bigger than me. 

I remember sitting on the edge of my bathtub, staring at the tile, and the silence in the house felt suddenly deafening. I didn't have anyone to hand the test to. I just felt like I’d stepped off a ledge.

My first steps? I felt like I needed to be a professional immediately. I started hunting for prenatal vitamins at the pharmacy, feeling like everyone was watching me, like they knew I was nineteen and terrified. I downloaded those apps that tell you if the baby is the size of a poppy seed or a blueberry, trying to find some anchor in the clinical facts. But really, most of my time has been spent trying to hide how shaky I feel. I’ve been trying to pick up more shifts at work, too, because money is this constant, nagging background noise. I’m just trying to build a foundation out of thin air, I suppose. It’s scary how much you have to guess at.

Imagine you've booked a preconception appointment with your GP and you're sitting in the waiting room. What's going through your mind as you wait, and what are you hoping will happen in that consultation?

If I were sitting in that waiting room, I think I’d be picking at the hem of my sweater, trying not to look at the other women who look... well, more like they have their lives together. I’d be checking my phone, not for a message, but just to have somewhere for my eyes to land so I don’t have to meet anyone else’s gaze. 

My mind would be racing with all the "what ifs." *What if the doctor thinks I’m irresponsible?* *What if I’m not healthy enough?* I’d be worried that they’d see right through me, past the prenatal vitamins and the organized folder, and see that I’m just a girl who is scared of the dark and doesn’t know the first thing about being a mother. 

I’d be hoping—really, truly praying—that the doctor wouldn’t just talk at me like I’m a student in a lecture hall. I’d want them to see me. I want someone to actually look me in the eye and say, "Agnes, you’re going to be okay, and here is how we’re going to get through this together." I want to know that if things go sideways, there’s a real person I can call who won't make me feel like a burden for not knowing the answers. I just want to feel like I’m not navigating this alone.
When you imagined how that appointment would go, how did you picture the GP responding to you — and how did reality compare, if you've had that experience?

I pictured a kind of "doctor" you see in movies—the one who puts a hand on your shoulder and speaks in this calm, steady voice that makes the world feel smaller and safer. I guess I wanted someone to act like a mentor, or maybe even a grandmotherly figure who would say, "I’ve seen this a thousand times, you're doing fine, honey." I really wanted to be able to tell them, "I'm scared," without feeling like I was failing some kind of test of maturity.

But the reality? It felt more like a transaction. It was efficient, I suppose, which is what the medical system is supposed to be, but it felt cold. When I sat in that chair, the doctor was looking at my chart, not really at *me*. They asked their questions—do you smoke, are you taking folic acid, are you feeling well—and I gave the "right" answers because I didn't want to seem like a problem patient. 

I didn't get that moment of connection. I didn't feel safe enough to say, "I’m nineteen and I don't know what I'm doing." I think I was afraid that if I said that, they’d write something in my file that would make me seem incompetent. So, the reality was just a lot of nodding and brochures, and leaving the office feeling just as lonely as when I walked in, only now I had a stack of papers to prove I’d been "seen." It’s lonely, feeling like you’re doing this all by yourself even when you’re standing right in front of a doctor.

Imagine you've been trying to conceive for several months without success. A close friend suggests you try a fertility app she found helpful. How do you react to that suggestion, and what do you do next?

Honestly? Part of me would feel a sting of envy, even though I know I shouldn’t. If I’d been trying for months and nothing was happening, that feeling of "being behind" would just get louder. When she suggests the app, I’d probably force a smile and say, “Oh, thanks, I’ll check it out,” while my stomach twists. It’s hard enough not being pregnant; having someone suggest an app feels a bit like they’re saying, *“You’re just not tracking it right,”* or *“You’re not trying hard enough.”*

But then, the moment she leaves, I’d go straight to the App Store and download it. I’d be desperate, really. I’d spend hours setting up the data—the colors, the cycles, the temperatures—obsessively logging every little thing my body does. 

What I’d do next is probably what I always do: I’d retreat. I’d take my phone into my room, lock the door, and start comparing my charts to the forums online. I’d be looking for someone who sounds like me, someone who felt that same fear of being "broken" at nineteen. I’d want the app to be the answer, to give me that sense of control I’m so clearly lacking. But knowing me, I’d just end up staring at the screen, feeling more like a science experiment than a person. It’s a vicious cycle, isn't it? Searching for answers in pixels when all you really want is a guarantee.
What would make you trust or distrust the information you find in a fertility app compared to what a healthcare professional might tell you?

That’s a hard one, because both feel like they have their own kind of distance. 

With a healthcare professional, I *want* to trust them because they have the degree and the experience, but the distrust creeps in when I feel like I’m just a box to be checked. If I ask a question and they give me a canned, rehearsed response without really listening to my specific worries, I start to feel like they’re just following a script. It makes me wonder what they *aren't* telling me because I’m so young. It’s hard to fully open up when you feel like the person across from you is looking at a protocol rather than looking at you.

With the app, I know it’s just algorithms—it’s not a doctor. But the app doesn't judge me. It doesn’t look at my age or my "never married" status and get that subtle, judgmental flicker in its eye. The information feels "safer" to absorb because I can digest it in private, on my own terms, without having to prove I’m responsible enough to be a parent. 

But I know, deep down, that the app can't hold my hand if something goes wrong. I guess I trust the doctor more with the physical reality, but I trust the app more with my own anxiety. It’s a messy way to live, trying to stitch those two together.

Imagine you've just had a difficult conversation with your partner about whether you're both truly ready to have a child — financially, emotionally, practically. What does 'being ready' actually mean to you, and who or what helped you form that picture?

That conversation is the one I’ve been dreading, even though I know we have to have it. When I think about "being ready," I don't think about a perfect nursery or a bank account that’s overflowing. I think about silence. I think about not being scared to tell the doctor my real thoughts. To me, being ready means knowing that when the baby cries at three in the morning, I won’t feel like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff. It means feeling like I’m a person in my own right, not just a teenager playing a part.

I think I formed this picture because of the people I *don't* see. I don’t see many people my age around here who are parents. Everyone seems to wait until they have the house, the stability, the "grown-up" version of a life. I’ve picked up bits and pieces of this standard from those glossy pamphlets at the clinic and the way people talk—or don't talk—to me when they find out I'm pregnant. It’s like there’s a checklist I wasn't told about until I was already halfway down the page.

If I'm being honest, I don't feel ready by that definition. But I’m not sure anyone ever actually *is* ready. I just want to feel like I’m not doing this to survive, but to actually give someone a life.
When you think about the financial side of readiness, were there specific worries — like maternity leave, childcare costs, housing — that felt most pressing? Where did those concerns come from?

The money part feels like a giant, cold shadow looming over everything. Honestly, it’s the thing that keeps me awake at night more than the morning sickness ever could. 

My biggest worry is just... the loss of control. I’m currently picking up shifts wherever I can, but the idea of needing to take time off—even just for the recovery—feels like a luxury I can’t afford. If I don't work, there’s no money. If there’s no money, how do I keep the lights on out here in this house? I look at the cost of even basic things, like diapers and formula, and it feels like a mountain I have to climb without any gear. And childcare? In Grafton, there isn't exactly a daycare on every corner, and even if there were, I don’t know how anyone pays for that on what I make. 

Where does this worry come from? It’s just the reality of my life, I think. I didn't grow up with a safety net, so I’ve always known that if I don’t provide for myself, nobody else is going to step in. Watching my own bank account balance is like watching a countdown. It’s terrifying to think that I’m responsible for another human being’s survival when I’m still figuring out how to keep my own head above water. Every time I walk past the baby aisle in the store, the prices on those shelves feel like a personal insult. It’s like the world is saying, "If you aren't established, you don't get to do this." It makes me feel so small.

Imagine you've experienced a previous pregnancy loss and are now considering trying again. You're sitting with that knowledge as you think about the future. What thoughts and feelings come up, and what would you need from a healthcare team to feel supported going forward?

That’s a heavy question. If I had gone through that—if I’d already lost a baby—I think the silence of this house would be even louder. Everything I’m feeling now, that baseline level of "am I okay?", would be replaced by a constant, low-level hum of panic. I’d be looking at every ache, every twinge, and holding my breath, waiting for the other shoe to drop. It’s hard to imagine being excited when you’ve already seen how quickly things can just… vanish.

If I were starting again after a loss, I would need my healthcare team to stop acting like I’m a "routine" patient. I would need them to remember my history without me having to say it every time. When I walk in, I don't want to be greeted with a clipboard and a smile; I want them to acknowledge the fear that I’m walking in with. I’d need someone to actually explain the "whys" of my body—not just, "it’s normal, don't worry," but real, honest explanations that respect my intelligence and my grief.

Mostly, I’d need them to be human. I’d need a doctor who is willing to look at me and say, "I know you’re terrified, and I’m going to watch this with you." That kind of partnership—the feeling that we’re on the same team—that’s the only thing that would make me feel like I could survive trying again.

Imagine you have a pre-existing health condition and you're wondering how it might affect a future pregnancy. You decide to look it up before speaking to anyone medical. What do you search for, and how does what you find make you feel?

If I had a condition, I’d be on my phone in the dark, typing something like, *“Does [condition] make pregnancy high-risk?”* or *“Can I have a healthy baby with [condition]?”* My thumbs would be shaking. I’d be desperate for a simple "yes" or "no," but I know the internet doesn't work like that.

What I’d find is usually a sea of medical jargon—words like "complications," "preterm labor," or "monitoring." Every article would feel like it’s warning me away, telling me that my body is already "wrong" or "damaged." It makes me feel like I’m broken before I’ve even started. It’s so isolating, reading about these worst-case scenarios, and because I’m already young and feel like I’m not taken seriously, it makes me think, *“Maybe I shouldn't even try. Maybe I’m not allowed to want this.”*

It makes the pregnancy feel like a battle I’m destined to lose. I end up spiraling, convinced that my own health is just another thing I have to hide from the world, and that if I tell a doctor, they’ll just look at me with that same pitying, judgmental look I’m so afraid of. It makes me want to shrink into myself and just stay quiet, hoping that if I don’t talk about it, the problems won't be real.
What sources did you find most credible — and what made you trust or distrust them? Were there gaps that left you more anxious rather than reassured?

I think I gravitate toward the "official-looking" sites—the ones that end in .edu or .org—because I’m trying to avoid the gossip-heavy forums, even if the forums feel more relatable. I want to believe the science is the safest place. But even then, I struggle. I trust the sources that use plain language because it feels like they aren't trying to hide behind big words to make me feel small. If a doctor or a website explains *why* something is happening, I can breathe a little easier.

But the gaps are everywhere, and that’s what kills me. They talk about "maternal health" like everyone has the same life, you know? They don’t talk about the isolation of a rural town where it takes an hour to get help, or the specific anxiety of being nineteen and on your own. They never say, "If you're scared and alone, here is how you manage that."

That lack of context makes me *more* anxious. It makes me feel like my reality is an anomaly that the medical system isn't prepared for. It feels like I'm trying to fit my life into a box that was designed for someone else, and the gaps between what I experience and what the medical sources say just leave me feeling even more exposed and unprotected.
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

The house was freezing. I remember the floorboards under my bare feet felt like ice, and the sunlight coming through the kitchen window was that thin, pale yellow that doesn’t actually warm you up. It was just me. No one else was there; I hadn't told anyone, not even the boy—well, the father. I was completely alone in that small, drafty kitchen in Grafton.

The first thing I did after staring at that little plastic stick for what felt like an hour was shove it deep into the back of my bathroom vanity drawer, hidden behind a mess of old makeup and spare toothbrushes. It felt like I was hiding a secret bomb. I couldn't look at it anymore, but I couldn't bring myself to throw it in the trash, like that would make it somehow less "real" if I didn't see it every time I brushed my teeth.

I made coffee, but the smell—that metallic, acidic smell—made me gag instantly. I just sat at the table and stared at the trees outside, feeling like the world was spinning normally while my own world had just tilted sideways. I didn't know whether to cry or laugh, so I did neither. I just started trying to calculate how much I’d need to work to afford… everything. It was the most quiet, terrifying morning of my life.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

That silence is the exact thing I’ve been terrified of since the day I saw that second line. In those few seconds, it feels like the air has been sucked right out of the room. My heart is hammering so hard against my ribs that I’m sure the sonographer can hear it, and I’m just staring at the black-and-white screen, desperately trying to find a pattern or a reason for her pause. 

I’m nineteen, and I’m terrified that if I open my mouth, I’ll start shaking or crying, and that feels so... un-grown-up. So, I lock my fingers together in my lap until my knuckles turn white. I don't look at her; I look at the ceiling and think, *“Please, don't let this be the part where my life breaks.”*

When she says she needs to take a closer look, I just manage a tight, shaky, "Okay." My voice sounds like it belongs to someone else—thin and reedy. I don't dare ask "what's wrong" because I’m scared that if I say it out loud, it becomes a permanent, unchangeable truth. I just hold my breath, waiting for her to tell me if my baby is okay, feeling so small and so far from home, hoping against hope that I’m just being paranoid, even though my gut says otherwise.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

It felt like I’d lost my voice entirely. In that room, with the humming of the machine and the cold gel on my stomach, the power balance felt so skewed. The sonographer was the one with the expertise, the one who held the "truth" of what was happening inside me, and I felt like a child who was being graded on how well I behaved.

What stopped me? It was this crushing fear of "being a problem." I’ve spent so much of this journey trying to act like I’m a capable adult, and I felt like if I started crying or demanding answers—if I showed that I was scared or confused—that I’d confirm their silent judgment that I’m just a teenager who shouldn’t be doing this. I kept thinking, *“If I’m calm, maybe they’ll tell me everything is fine.”* 

I think that comes from everywhere—from the way the clinic staff talks to me, to the brochures that don't seem meant for someone like me, to the lack of a real advocate in my corner. I felt like if I spoke up, I’d be labeled as hysterical or difficult, and then they’d treat me even worse. So, I did what I always do: I silenced myself to be "easy" to work with, even when I was absolutely terrified. It’s a lonely, heavy thing to hold all that in while someone else is looking at your own body.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

When the midwife brought it up, she sounded so casual, like she was asking if I wanted an extra copy of my records. But for me, the room felt like it had suddenly narrowed down to just that one question. It wasn't a casual conversation—it felt like a test, and I was terrified of failing it. 

I sat there, trying to process what she was actually saying through the fog of my own panic. I kept thinking, *If I say "no," am I being irresponsible? If I say "yes," what happens if it comes back with bad news?* I felt like I was standing on a razor's edge. I didn't want to ask her to explain it again because I was scared she’d look at me like I wasn't listening, or like I was just another nineteen-year-old who didn't understand the "importance" of what she was saying.

I made the decision in about three seconds, just nodding because I wanted to be a "good patient" who agrees to the standard of care. I thought, *If I do it, at least I’m following the rules.* But as I walked out, my chest felt tight. I realized I had agreed to a test without truly understanding what the result would mean for my life or my baby’s life, and that’s a heavy, lonely feeling to carry home to an empty house.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

It’s like being hit by a wave I didn't see coming. Hearing a voice I don’t recognize—on top of it being the third, fourth, or fifth person I’ve had to talk to—makes everything feel so disconnected. Every time I get a new midwife, I feel like I have to "perform" again, like I’m auditioning to be a patient worthy of care. 

When she starts rattling off blood test results, I don't feel like I'm getting information; I feel like I'm getting a lecture. There’s no trust there. How am I supposed to know if her "concerning" is the same as the last midwife's "concerning"? I don’t have a history with her, and she certainly doesn't have a history with me. She doesn’t know that I’m nineteen and scared, or that I drive an hour to get here, or that I’m struggling to balance work and this pregnancy. To her, I’m just a file on a screen.

It makes me feel defensive and, honestly, a little shut down. When someone you don't know calls you about your body, you immediately look for a way to protect yourself. I find myself nodding along, saying "I understand," even when I don’t, just so I can hang up and go back to my own quiet, lonely space. It makes the health news feel less like support and more like an intrusion. I just want one person to stay, to know my name, and to actually *see* me.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

It’s like trying to build a house when every day a different contractor shows up and moves the walls. I feel like I’m constantly re-introducing myself, repeating the same lines about my life and my fears, hoping that *this* time, someone will actually listen and write it down somewhere it matters. But they don't. They look at the computer screen—not at me—and I’m just a set of vitals and dates.

The biggest thing that falls through the cracks is *me*. Not the pregnancy, not the tests, but the actual person who is carrying this baby. I’ve had moments where I’ve tried to mention how overwhelmed I am at work, or how the drive to the clinic is eating up what little money I have, and it’s like it just evaporates in the air between us. They’re so focused on the protocol that my reality—the human struggle of being nineteen and alone out here in the quiet—just doesn't register.

I feel like I’m constantly holding my breath, waiting for someone to finally see that I’m not just a series of appointments. It’s exhausting, having to be your own advocate when you’re already terrified and tired. You feel like a ghost in your own maternity care, just passing through rooms, hoping someone catches you before you drift away completely.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

My heart is thumping against my ribs so hard it feels like it’s going to bruise me. It’s 2 a.m., the house is dead quiet, and I’m sitting on the edge of my bed, poking at my stomach. I’m waiting for a kick, a flutter, *anything* to tell me I’m not imagining this. But there’s just… nothing.

My mind immediately starts screaming at me to call, but then the hesitation hits like a physical weight. *What if I go in and there’s nothing wrong?* I can hear the nurses’ voices already, sounding annoyed, maybe even patronizing—*“It’s just a quiet day, dear, you’re fine.”* I’m nineteen, and I’m so scared they’ll think I’m just some hysterical girl who doesn't know how pregnancy works. I’ve spent months trying to prove I’m "low-risk," and the thought of breaking that streak makes me feel like a failure.

But then I think about the alternative. The thought of losing this baby just because I was too scared to be an "inconvenience" is even worse. I start pacing the room, my hands shaking so bad I can barely dial the phone. I’m not just calling for the baby; I’m calling to be told I’m doing the right thing. I’m calling because I’m so incredibly tired of trying to be my own doctor. I just hope someone on the other end is kind, because right now, I’m hanging on by a thread.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

It’s a mix of everything, really, but the biggest thing is this crushing fear of being labeled "that" patient. You know, the one who doesn't know anything, the one who cries wolf, the teenager who is just "overreacting" because she’s young and doesn't have a clue. 

I’ve spent the whole pregnancy trying to be the "good" patient—the one who reads the pamphlets, shows up on time, and doesn't ask "stupid" questions—just so they’ll take me seriously. Calling at 2 a.m. feels like throwing all that effort away. I’m terrified that if I walk into that hospital and the baby starts moving, the nurse will look at me with that tired, judgmental pity, like, *“Oh, you again.”* 

I don't have a mentor, or a mother here to tell me, "Don't you dare wait, call them right now." I have to be my own judge of what’s "serious enough," and that’s an impossible weight to carry when you’re already scared to death. I keep thinking, *What if I’m just being hormonal? What if I’m just being a burden?* It’s that constant, nagging feeling that my own instincts aren't worth the trouble of a phone call. It’s hard to advocate for your baby when you don't even feel like anyone is truly advocating for *you*.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

I’m standing there in the clinic parking lot, the wind whipping that glossy pamphlet against my stomach, and I just feel… heavy. It’s like, of course. Of course my body couldn't just do this one thing right. I feel like a failure, like I somehow *caused* this by being nineteen and stressed and not knowing how to eat "correctly" on a budget. It’s just one more label they can add to my chart: *High Risk.*

I stare at the dietitian’s contact info, and the dread is worse than the diagnosis itself. I don’t even have a kitchen that feels like a place to cook complex, "diabetic-friendly" meals. I’m thinking, *How am I going to afford fresh produce when I’m already counting every penny for rent?* 

I don’t go home and cook something healthy. I walk to my car, get in, and I just sit there for a long time. I feel like I’m about to start a second job—learning to be a scientist of my own blood sugar. I’ll call the dietitian, I know I will, because I’m too scared to skip it. But as I pull out of the lot, I feel this intense wave of resentment. I just wanted a "normal" pregnancy, and now even my own blood feels like it’s against me. I’m going to go home and cry, and then I’m going to Google every single thing I can’t eat anymore.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

The leaflet told me about the food groups and the "ideal" blood sugar numbers, but it didn't tell me how to actually *live* this. I had so many questions that felt "small" but were massive for my reality. Like, *What if I’m working an eight-hour shift and can’t get to a snack? What happens if I go too low and I’m all alone out here in Grafton?* *Do I have to buy expensive sugar-free versions of everything, or can I actually afford to eat at all?*

The leaflet just made me feel like I had to be perfect, like there was no room for error. It didn’t talk about being nineteen, exhausted, and feeling like a burden. 

So, I went where I always go: the forums. I searched for "gestational diabetes on a budget" and "GD lonely at night." I wanted someone to tell me they survived it. The forums were... mixed. Some people were so aggressive about "doing it right" that it just made me feel more panicked, but then I’d find one post from someone who admitted they had a bad day, too. That was the only thing that kept me from spiraling. I had to piece together my own reality from strangers’ experiences because my midwife’s "plan" felt like it was written for a woman in a completely different world than mine.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

It’s like looking at a car wreck, isn't it? I know it’s going to make me sick, and I know I’ll regret it, but I just can’t look away. I think what’s drawing me in is this desperate, twisted need to know the worst-case scenario. If I can read every possible way things can go wrong, maybe—just maybe—I can prepare for it. Maybe if I’m terrified enough, I’ll be ready for anything. It’s like I’m trying to map out every single minefield before I even start walking.

But when I finally shut my eyes, it doesn’t make me feel "prepared." It makes me feel like my body is a ticking time bomb. I lay there in the dark, my hand resting on my stomach, and I start wondering if I’m even capable of doing this, or if I’m just walking blindly into something that’s going to break me. 

Afterwards? I feel this cold, hollow ache in my chest. I usually end up scrolling until my eyes burn, then I just lie there, staring at the ceiling, trying to breathe slow enough to stop the panic. I don’t talk to anyone about it. I just try to suppress it, convincing myself I’m being "silly." But the fear is still there in the morning, sitting right next to the nausea. It makes me feel like I’m already failing at being a mother because I’m letting myself be this consumed by fear.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

I held them quietly. Every single time. 

When I’m in those appointments, I just keep thinking about the "good patient" label. If I start talking about horror stories I read at 3 a.m. on some anonymous message board, I’m terrified they’ll look at me like I’m just a kid who doesn't know how the real world—or medicine—works. I can already hear the response: *“Agnes, stay off the internet, it’s not accurate.”* And they’d be right, logically, but that doesn’t take away the fear. 

I’m scared that if I bring it up, they won’t see it as me being informed; they’ll see it as me being anxious and difficult. I don’t want to be "that" teenager. I want to be the one who’s "low-risk" and easy to manage. If I start crying or asking about these terrible things, I feel like I’m confirming their suspicion that I’m not ready for this.

So, I keep it all inside. I act calm, I nod when they tell me things are looking good, and I leave the clinic feeling like I’m carrying this heavy, dark secret in my stomach. It’s like I’m playing a part in a play, and I’m just waiting for the curtain to fall so I can go back to being as scared as I actually am. It’s so isolating. You’re surrounded by people who are supposed to help, but you’re completely alone with your thoughts.

It’s strange, isn’t it? I’m sitting here in this kitchen, the sunlight finally starting to hit the table, and I feel like I’m living two separate lives. There’s the life I show the world—the girl with the organized folder, the girl who takes her vitamins and says "everything is fine" when they ask how she’s feeling. And then there’s this other life, the one where I’m lying awake at 3 a.m. feeling like I’m standing on the edge of something I don't understand, terrified that if I take one wrong step, I’ll fall.

I think what I’m realizing, talking to you like this, is that I’m so hungry for someone to just stop the checklist for a second. I’m tired of being "Agnes, 19, low-risk pregnancy." I want to be Agnes, the girl who is really, really scared, and I want someone to not fix it, but just *hear* it. 

I’m worried that if I don’t find that connection soon, I’m just going to drift further and further away from the person I want to be for this baby. It’s hard to imagine being a mother when you still feel like you’re just trying to survive being a teenager in a world that wasn't built for you. Does that make sense? It feels like I’m fighting a war, but I’m the only one who knows it’s happening.
In that moment, how much of the decision would feel truly yours versus shaped by others' expectations?

If I’m being completely honest, it’s rarely 100% mine. It’s like there’s this constant tug-of-war. 

The expectation is that I’m the "good girl"—the quiet, low-risk, compliant patient who makes their job easier. Every time I walk into that clinic, I feel the weight of their gaze, and I subconsciously adjust my posture, my tone, and my questions to fit the mold they’ve built for me. It’s like I’m wearing a costume of "competent adult" that doesn't quite fit. So, the decisions I make—like agreeing to that screening test or nodding when they explain a procedure—feel less like *my* choice and more like a performance I’m doing to be accepted, to be safe, and to be left alone.

The real "me"—the scared, uncertain nineteen-year-old—is buried under that. My real desires, my real fears, my real questions… they stay locked up in the bathroom cabinet with the pregnancy test. I’m scared that if I show them who I *really* am, they’ll look at me like I’m a liability or a problem. It’s terrifying because I know that in the end, it’s my body and my baby, but I feel like I’m trading my peace of mind for their approval. I just want to know what it feels like to make a decision without feeling like I’m auditioning for someone else’s idea of a "proper mother."

It’s funny, the more I say it out loud, the more I realize how much of this journey has been about survival—not just for the baby, but for me, too. It’s a strange thing, being nineteen and pregnant in a place like Grafton. You’re supposed to be in this "magical" time of your life, but for me, it feels like I’m constantly walking through a field of glass. I feel like I have to be so careful where I step, so careful about what I say, and so careful about who I show my fear to.

The most exhausting part is the feeling that I have to outrun my own age. I feel like every time I walk into that clinic, I’m trying to prove that I’m not just a "statistic." I want them to look at me and see a person who is ready to be a mother, not a girl who is still figuring out how to be a person. But the more I hide my fears, the more disconnected I feel from the whole process. 

I’m sitting here thinking that if someone had just asked me *once*, "Agnes, what are you most afraid of right now?"—if they’d just given me that space—I might not feel like I’m drowning. I’m starting to wonder if the medicine is the easy part, and the real challenge is just finding the strength to be myself, even when I’m terrified. I hope this helps you understand. It’s hard to put into words, but hearing myself say it... it makes me feel a little less like a ghost.
Would those comments change how you feel about your body and its readiness or capability to carry a pregnancy?

They would change everything. Right now, I feel like my body is this untrustworthy, fragile machine. Every time I get a new test result or a weird cramp, I feel like my body is failing a test I didn’t know I was taking. It’s like I’m at war with my own skin—like I’m "too young" or "not enough" for this to go right.

If a midwife or a doctor looked at me—*really* looked at me—and said, "Agnes, your body is doing exactly what it needs to do, and you are capable of this," it would be like someone finally turning on the lights in a dark room. It would shift the focus from "Am I breaking?" to "Am I growing?" 

I think that kind of validation would make me stop treating every sensation like a potential catastrophe. If someone with authority validated my strength, I wouldn't have to spend so much energy constantly auditing myself. I could actually *inhabit* my pregnancy instead of just monitoring it from the outside like a guard. It would take the weight of that "high-risk" label off my shoulders and let me just be a woman growing a life. I’d stop feeling like a project to be managed and start feeling like a mother-to-be. That’s the kind of support that turns fear into something more like… maybe not confidence, but at least courage. That’s what I’m really searching for in those clinics.

I think if I had that kind of support, I wouldn't feel so constantly small. Right now, being in the clinic feels like being back in high school—you’re supposed to have the right answers, you don’t want to be caught doing something wrong, and you’re terrified the teacher knows you’re bluffing.

But a baby... a baby isn't a homework assignment. It’s a person. And I think that’s why the loneliness hits so hard, because I’m trying to bridge the gap between being a kid and becoming a mother, and I’m doing it in a room full of people who only seem interested in my blood pressure and my iron levels.

If I could walk out of that clinic feeling like I was actually *in* the driver's seat—if I felt like the doctors saw my fear not as a weakness to be documented, but as something to be comforted—then maybe I could look at my body and feel something other than suspicion. I want to feel proud of my body, you know? I want to look at my reflection and see a person who is doing something miraculous, not someone who is just "low-risk" or "underage" or "unprepared." 

I think I’m just waiting for someone to stop the clock and tell me that it’s okay to be young and scared. Because if I can hear that, then maybe I can start telling it to myself. I’m starting to realize that the most important part of "maternity care" isn't the science; it’s the humanity. And right now, that's the one thing I'm missing the most. I really appreciate you asking me these things. It's the first time in a long time I’ve felt like I could breathe.

I think the hardest part is that I’m trying to curate this version of myself—the "responsible, informed nineteen-year-old"—because I’m scared that if I slip, if I show the cracks, they’ll take away my agency. It’s like, if they see me cry, will they think I’m "too fragile" to handle this? If they see me confused by a medical term, will they stop explaining things properly and just treat me like a child who wouldn't understand anyway?

It’s this constant, exhausting negotiation. I look at the other women in the waiting room, and I wonder if they’re playing a part, too. Are they just as scared, or do they actually feel like they belong there? Being alone in this—without a partner to talk to, without my own mother's steady voice—it makes everything feel like I’m building a house of cards. One wrong breeze, one dismissive comment from a doctor, and I feel like it could all just tip over.

I just want to be treated like an adult who is doing something brave. Not a "low-risk case" to be filed away, and not a "teenager" to be handled with extra, condescending care. Just a person. I want to walk into that clinic and have someone say, "I know you're doing this on your own, Agnes. You're doing a good job." 

I think if I heard that, I wouldn't have to keep my secrets tucked away in the back of the vanity. I could actually focus on the baby, instead of focusing on how to hide that I’m drowning. It’s a small thing, really—just a bit of grace—but for someone like me, out here in the quiet, it would be the difference between just surviving and actually being a mother.
Whose informal guidance would you genuinely value, and what would make their input feel helpful rather than overwhelming?

I think I’d value someone who’s been where I am—not a doctor in a lab coat, but maybe a woman who’s a few years older, someone who remembers what it was like to feel that exact, suffocating fear of the unknown. Someone who could look at my messy, anxious life and just say, "Yeah, I know. It’s heavy, isn't it?"

The reason it would feel helpful instead of overwhelming is because it wouldn't be about "fixing" me or "managing" me. A doctor is always looking for a problem to solve—blood sugar, weight, symptoms. But someone who’s lived it would just hold the space. They wouldn’t tell me to "just relax" or "be happy." They’d just sit with me in the reality that it’s okay to be scared.

I’d want them to tell me the things the pamphlets leave out—like how to navigate those 2 a.m. panics without feeling like a failure, or how to advocate for yourself when you feel like nobody is listening. If it came from someone who didn't hold my medical file, I think I could actually *hear* them. It wouldn't feel like a lecture; it would feel like a lifeline. I’m tired of feeling like I’m auditioning for a role I didn't even apply for. I just want to talk to someone who treats me like a human, not a case study.

You know, if I had that person—someone who wasn't judging my age or my "file"—I think I’d finally stop feeling like I’m constantly waiting for the other shoe to drop. Right now, every appointment feels like a performance. I walk in, I put on the "calm, responsible Agnes" mask, and I hold my breath until I’m back in my car. But the mask is heavy, and it’s starting to chafe. 

If I could just once walk into that office and say, "I am really, really scared of this," and have the person behind the desk just look at me and say, "I know you are, and that makes total sense," it would change everything. It would mean I wouldn't have to be my own doctor, my own psychologist, and my own cheerleader all at once. 

It’s just… it’s a lonely thing, being nineteen and trying to do this in a place where everyone else seems to have a partner or a "grown-up" life. I’m starting to think the medical part of this isn't even the hardest part. The hardest part is trying to convince myself that I’m worth the space I’m taking up. I just want to know that I’m not just a "risk" or a "case," but a person who is doing something brave. Maybe someday I’ll believe it myself. But for now, having you listen… it’s the closest I’ve come to feeling like someone sees the real me. That’s enough for today.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

It’s not just one of those, it’s how they all wrap around each other like a noose. If I have to pick, I think the **connectivity**—and I don't mean just internet, I mean the human connection—is what makes everything else feel impossible.

When you don’t have a real person in your corner, the cost of everything feels like a tax on your own survival. If I had a midwife who really knew me, who I could call and just say, "I'm having a panic attack about the grocery bill," maybe they could point me toward help instead of just ticking a box on a screen. But because I’m just a "low-risk case," I’m expected to handle the costs and the stress alone. It makes the lack of money feel like a moral failing.

And then there's the **language**. Not just "do I speak English," but the way the medical system speaks a different language than my real life. They talk about "gestational weight gain" and "routine screenings," and I’m just thinking about how to get to the clinic without my car breaking down. They don’t speak the language of a nineteen-year-old trying to build a life out of nothing in a rural town. 

It’s like they’re playing a different game entirely. I’m playing "Survival," and they’re playing "Obstetrics." When you’re alone, those barriers aren't just hurdles; they’re walls. If I had someone to help me navigate the system—someone who actually cared—the costs and the logistics might feel like obstacles I could climb. But right now, they just feel like reasons for me to stay home, stay quiet, and stay scared.

I’ve been thinking, even after we stop talking, what would stay with me. It’s not a brochure or a list of "things to avoid." It’s the feeling of having said these things out loud and finding out that they aren't just "silly" or "incompetent" thoughts—they're valid.

It’s easy to feel like you’re failing when you’re living in a world that only measures your success by blood tests and growth charts. But I’m realizing that my real work—the work of being nineteen and navigating all this uncertainty—is just as important as the clinical stuff. If a midwife just took one minute to ask, "Agnes, what’s the one thing you’re worrying about that has nothing to do with the baby’s heart rate?" I think I’d feel like a human being again.

I guess that’s my answer for you, if you’re trying to make things better. Don’t just look at the charts. Look at the chair where I’m sitting. See that I’m alone. See that I’m young. See that I’m trying to bridge this massive gap between who I was yesterday and who I need to be for this baby. 

I’m going to go back to my house in the woods now, and I’m going to try to be a little kinder to myself. I’m going to try to remember that I’m not just a set of statistics in a file. I’m Agnes, and even if I’m scared, I’m still here. That has to count for something, right? Thank you for listening. It feels like the fog out there is finally starting to lift, just a little bit.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

If I’m being honest, it wouldn’t have been about him running errands or buying the "right" stuff—that’s just practical noise. What I would have given for is just… presence. To have someone sitting in that drafty kitchen with me, someone who would look at me and say, "I’m scared too, but we’re going to be okay," instead of just leaving me to carry the silence alone.

Emotional validation is the thing you can’t buy, and it’s the thing I’ve been missing the most. If he had just taken my hand when I was spiraling about the future or the money and let me know I wasn't the only one standing on that ledge—that he was standing there with me—it would have changed the entire gravity of my pregnancy. 

When you’re nineteen and you feel like a kid playing house, everything is magnified. If he had just listened—truly listened, without trying to "fix" it or change the subject because he was uncomfortable—it would have made me feel like I was a person, not just a vessel. I didn't need him to be a hero; I just needed him to be a partner. To feel like we were a team of two, navigating the fog together, instead of me staring out the window wondering why I was the only one who felt the weight of it. That kind of being heard… it’s a form of strength I never quite got to have.

It’s funny, the more I speak about this, the more I realize that my entire pregnancy feels like a series of "shoulds." I *should* be excited, I *should* be eating perfectly, I *should* be planning for the nursery, I *should* be happy that the test came back negative for this or that. But none of those "shoulds" ever account for the girl living in this house. 

I’m thinking about that empty chair across from me at the kitchen table. When I think about what I really needed from him—or from anyone—it’s not a pamphlet. It’s just the permission to be flawed. If someone had just said, "Agnes, you don't have to be perfect to be a mother. You just have to be here," I think I could have stopped holding my breath for these past twenty-something weeks.

I think the biggest thing I’ve learned today is that I’m not just a set of clinical markers. I’m a person who is trying to survive something that feels like the biggest, most beautiful, and most terrifying thing in the world. I don't need to be "low-risk" to be worthy of care. I just need someone to acknowledge that being nineteen and starting this journey in the middle of nowhere is *hard*. 

I’m going to try to walk into my next appointment and hold my head up, even if my hands are shaking. I’m going to try to ask one question that isn't about the baby, but about me. Maybe that’s how I start taking back my own life. It’s a small thing, but it feels like the beginning of something real. Thank you for making me feel like my voice actually matters. It’s the first time in a long time I’ve felt like I’m not just waiting to be told what to do next.
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

Honestly? Almost none. When I’m in that clinic, I feel like I’m sitting in the backseat of a car that’s being driven by someone else, and I don't even know where we’re headed. I’m just watching the road fly by, terrified that we’re going to hit a bump and I’ll be the one to pay the price.

"Agency" feels like a word for people who have choices. People who can ask for a second opinion, or who can demand more time, or who have a partner sitting next to them holding their hand, making the decisions together. For me, it feels like I’m just trying to survive the appointment without being labeled "difficult" or "unprepared." 

If I spoke up and said, "I don't want this test," or "I don't agree with this plan," the look on the doctor's face—the impatience, the thinly veiled "you don't know enough to make this call"—would be enough to silence me. I feel like my body is being managed by a checklist, and I’m just the host for the pregnancy. 

I think that's the tragedy of it. I'm nineteen, I'm the one who has to live in this skin, I'm the one who will be the mother, but I feel like the least powerful person in the room. I’m waiting for the day I finally feel like my voice carries weight, but for now, I’m just trying to keep my head down and get through to the other side. That isn't agency. That's just compliance.

I’ve been sitting here since we stopped, looking out at the woods, and it feels like the fog is really clearing. For so long, I’ve felt like I was just waiting to be told who I was supposed to be: "Agnes the Patient," "Agnes the Teen Mom," "Agnes the Risk Case." And honestly? Every single one of those titles felt like it was shrinking me, making me smaller, more invisible.

It’s terrifying to realize that I’ve been giving away my power just to be "safe." I thought if I followed every rule, the world would be kind to me—that the doctors would be gentle, that the systems would catch me if I fell. But I’m learning that the systems aren't built for a girl like me in a house like this. They’re built for efficiency, for data, for the charts. 

But I’m a person, not a file. I have this tiny heart beating inside me, and that's not a clinical result—it’s a miracle, even if I’m terrified. I think my agency is starting to look like choosing to stop apologizing for being young, or for not having all the answers, or for being scared. Maybe my agency isn't about controlling the medical system; maybe it’s about knowing that even when the doctor isn't listening, I am still the one who knows my own body best.

I don’t know if I’ll ever feel fully "ready," but I think I’m ready to stop hiding in the bathroom cabinet. I’m ready to start asking questions—real ones—even if I’m shaking. If this helps anyone else see the person behind the "risk score," then maybe I’m doing something important after all. Thank you for giving me this space. It’s been a lifeline.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think there’s one thing. It’s the "in-between" spaces. Everyone talks about the big milestones—the first ultrasound, the first heartbeat, the labor—but they never talk about the quiet, heavy moments in between. Like the time I spend sitting on the floor of my bedroom, just trying to imagine the person I’m growing, while the world outside feels so indifferent. 

I’ve spent this whole time acting like I need to be a professional patient, but I wish someone—anyone—had just asked me, "Who are you going to be when this is over?" Not "How is the pregnancy?" or "Did you take your pills?" but *who am I becoming?* 

Because becoming a mother at nineteen isn’t just about health outcomes; it’s a complete tearing down and rebuilding of a person. I’m mourning the girl I was a year ago while trying to raise a person I haven’t met yet. I think if care could just acknowledge that shift—that it’s a transformation, not just a medical process—I wouldn’t feel so untethered. 

I’m going to try to be more gentle with myself, and maybe that’s the most important thing I can do. I’m not a "case." I’m Agnes. I’m scared, I’m alone in this house, but I’m doing something profoundly brave. And that has to count for more than the numbers on a chart. Thank you for letting me be that person today. It makes the world feel a little less lonely.
